title: The Great Gatsby
author: F. Scott Fitzgerald
description: A timeless story of wealth, love, and the American Dream set in the roaring 1920s. Nick Carraway narrates the mysterious life of his neighbour Jay Gatsby, whose obsession with Daisy Buchanan leads to tragedy.
preview: In my younger and more vulnerable years my father gave me some advice that I've been turning over in my mind ever since.
fullText:
Chapter 1

In my younger and more vulnerable years my father gave me some advice that I've been turning over in my mind ever since. 'Whenever you feel like criticizing anyone,' he told me, 'just remember that all the people in this world haven't had the advantages that you've had.'

Chapter 2

About half way between West Egg and New York the motor road hastily joins the railroad and runs beside it for a quarter of a mile, so as to shrink away from a certain desolate area of land. This is a valley of ashes — a fantastic farm where ashes grow like wheat into ridges and hills.

Chapter 3

There was music from my neighbour's house through the summer nights. In his blue gardens men and girls came and went like moths among the whisperings and the champagne and the stars. At high tide in the afternoon I watched his guests diving from the tower of his raft, or taking the sun on the hot sand of his beach.

title: To Kill a Mockingbird
author: Harper Lee
description: A powerful novel about racial injustice and moral growth in the American South, told through the eyes of young Scout Finch as her father Atticus defends a Black man falsely accused of a crime.
preview: When he was nearly thirteen, my brother Jem got his arm badly broken at the elbow.
fullText:
Chapter 1

When he was nearly thirteen, my brother Jem got his arm badly broken at the elbow. When it healed, and Jem's fears of never being able to play football were assuaged, he was seldom self-conscious about his injury.

Chapter 2

The Radley Place jutted into a sharp curve beyond our house. The house was low, was once white with a deep front porch and green shutters, but had long ago darkened to the color of the slate-gray yard around it.

Chapter 3

Miss Caroline Fisher was no more than twenty-one. She had bright auburn hair, pink cheeks, and wore crimson fingernail polish. She also carried a card that was unfamiliar to me but appeared to be a new type of teaching aid.

author: George Orwell
description: A haunting vision of a totalitarian society where Big Brother watches every move. Winston Smith struggles to maintain his humanity in a world where truth itself is controlled by the ruling Party.
preview: It was a bright cold day in April, and the clocks were striking thirteen.
fullText:
Chapter 1

It was a bright cold day in April, and the clocks were striking thirteen. Winston Smith, his chin nuzzled into his breast in an effort to escape the vile wind, slipped quickly through the glass doors of Victory Mansions.

The hallway smelt of boiled cabbage and old rag mats. At one end of it a coloured poster, too large for the hallway, had been tacked to the wall. It depicted simply an enormous face — BIG BROTHER IS WATCHING YOU.

Chapter 2

As Winston walked up the stairs, he had a vague, intermittent memory of something that had happened a few years ago — or had it been much longer? He could never fix the precise moment when it had happened.

title: Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone
author: J.K. Rowling
description: The magical beginning of Harry Potter's journey as he discovers he is a wizard and enrolls at Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry, uncovering a destiny tied to the dark wizard Voldemort.
preview: Mr. and Mrs. Dursley, of number four, Privet Drive, were proud to say that they were perfectly normal, thank you very much.
fullText:
Chapter 1: The Boy Who Lived

Mr. and Mrs. Dursley, of number four, Privet Drive, were proud to say that they were perfectly normal, thank you very much. They were the last people you'd expect to be involved in anything strange or mysterious.

Chapter 2: The Vanishing Glass

Nearly ten years had passed since the Dursleys had woken up to find their nephew on the front step, but Privet Drive had hardly changed at all. The sun rose on the same tidy front gardens and lit up the brass number four on the Dursleys' front door.

title: The Alchemist
author: Paulo Coelho
description: A philosophical novel about a young Andalusian shepherd's journey to find treasure, discovering that the real treasure lies in following your Personal Legend and listening to your heart.
preview: The boy's name was Santiago. Dusk was falling as the boy arrived with his herd at an abandoned church.
fullText:
Prologue

The alchemist picked up a book that someone in the caravan had brought. Leafing through the pages, he found a story about Narcissus.

Part One

The boy's name was Santiago. Dusk was falling as the boy arrived with his herd at an abandoned church. The roof had fallen in long ago, and an enormous sycamore had grown up where the sacristy had once stood. He decided to spend the night there.

He tied up his sheep, cleared away the dry leaves from the floor, and lay down, using his book as a pillow. He told himself that he would have to start reading thicker books — they lasted longer and made more comfortable pillows.

title: Atomic Habits
author: James Clear
description: A revolutionary guide to building good habits and breaking bad ones. A proven framework for every day improvement with tiny changes that deliver remarkable results over time.
preview: The fate of British Cycling changed one day in 2003 when performance director Dave Brailsford was hired.
fullText:
Introduction: The Surprising Power of Atomic Habits

The fate of British Cycling changed one day in 2003 when the performance director Dave Brailsford was hired.

Chapter 1: The Surprising Power of Atomic Habits

It is so easy to overestimate the importance of one defining moment and underestimate the value of making small improvements on a daily basis. Too often, we convince ourselves that massive success requires massive action.

Chapter 2: How Your Habits Shape Your Identity

There are three levels of change: outcome change, process change, and identity change. The most effective way to change your habits is to focus not on what you want to achieve, but on who you wish to become.

title: The Da Vinci Code
author: Dan Brown
description: A thrilling mystery involving secret societies, hidden codes in famous artworks, and a conspiracy that could shake the foundations of Christianity. A breathless race across Europe.
preview: Renowned curator Jacques Saunière staggered through the vaulted archway of the museum's Grand Gallery.
fullText:
Prologue

Renowned curator Jacques Saunière staggered through the vaulted archway of the museum's Grand Gallery. He lunged for the nearest painting he could see, a Caravaggio. Grabbing the gilded frame, he heaved the masterpiece toward himself.

Chapter 1

Harvard symbologist Robert Langdon awoke suddenly to the shrill of his bedside phone. 'Mr. Langdon?' The man's accent was not easy to place. 'I apologize for disturbing you at this hour. I need your help with something. A murder, Mr. Langdon.'

Chapter 2

Sophie Neveu arrived at the Louvre within minutes. She was thirty-two, with auburn hair and green eyes. She worked for the French Cryptology Department and she had a message for Langdon that nobody else could see.

title: Rich Dad Poor Dad
author: Robert T. Kiyosaki
description: What the rich teach their kids about money that the poor and middle class do not. A personal finance classic that challenges conventional wisdom about money and building wealth.
preview: I had two fathers, a rich one and a poor one.
fullText:
Introduction

I had two fathers, a rich one and a poor one. One was highly educated and intelligent; he had a Ph.D. The other father never finished the eighth grade.

Chapter 1: Rich Dad, Poor Dad

My two dads had opposing attitudes in thought. One dad thought that the rich should pay more in taxes to take care of those less fortunate. The other said, 'Taxes punish those who produce and reward those who don't produce.'

Chapter 2: The Rich Don't Work for Money

The poor and the middle class work for money. The rich have money work for them. If you work for money, you give the power to your employer. If money works for you, you keep the power.

title: Sapiens
author: Yuval Noah Harari
description: A brief history of humankind from the Stone Age to the present day. Harari explores how Homo sapiens came to dominate the world and what it means for our future.
preview: About 13.5 billion years ago, matter, energy, time and space came into being in what is known as the Big Bang.
fullText:
Part One: The Cognitive Revolution

About 13.5 billion years ago, matter, energy, time and space came into being in what is known as the Big Bang. The story of these fundamental features of our universe is called physics.

Chapter 1: An Animal of No Significance

About 3.8 billion years ago, on a planet called Earth, certain molecules combined to form particularly large and intricate structures called organisms.

Some 70,000 years ago, organisms belonging to the species Homo sapiens started to form even more elaborate structures called cultures. The subsequent development of these human cultures is called history.

title: The Hobbit
author: J.R.R. Tolkien
description: The beloved tale of Bilbo Baggins swept into an epic quest to reclaim the lost Dwarf Kingdom of Erebor from the fearsome dragon Smaug — the adventure that started it all.
preview: In a hole in the ground there lived a hobbit. Not a nasty, dirty, wet hole, filled with the ends of worms and an oozy smell.
fullText:
Chapter 1: An Unexpected Party

In a hole in the ground there lived a hobbit. Not a nasty, dirty, wet hole, filled with the ends of worms and an oozy smell, nor yet a dry, bare, sandy hole with nothing in it to sit down on or to eat: it was a hobbit-hole, and that means comfort.

It had a perfectly round door like a porthole, painted green, with a shiny yellow brass knob in the exact middle. The door opened on to a tube-shaped hall like a tunnel: a very comfortable tunnel without smoke, with panelled walls, and floors tiled and carpeted, furnished with polished chairs, and lots and lots of pegs for hats and coats.

Chapter 2: Roast Mutton

Up they got, and had breakfast as quickly as they could, taking only what they could carry and what they needed most; and they set off, leaving the supper things unwashed, beds unmade, and everything as it was. There was no time for Bilbo to think of tidying up.

title: Pride and Prejudice
author: Jane Austen
description: The beloved story of Elizabeth Bennet and Mr. Darcy — whose initial misunderstandings and prejudices evolve into a romance that has captivated readers for over two centuries.
preview: It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife.
fullText:
Chapter 1

It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife.

'My dear Mr. Bennet,' said his lady to him one day, 'have you heard that Netherfield Park is let at last?'

Mr. Bennet replied that he had not.

'But it is,' returned she; 'for Mrs. Long has just been here, and she told me all about it.'

Chapter 2

Mr. Bennet was among the earliest of his neighbours in calling upon Mr. Bingley. In the course of the evening the younger Miss Bennets heard much to their advantage from their friend Miss Lucas. Jane ran away with all their admiration.

title: The Midnight Library
author: Matt Haig
description: Between life and death there is a library. A magical story about all the lives we could have lived — and what makes life truly worth living.
preview: Nineteen years before she decided to die, Nora Seed sat in the warmth of the school music room.
fullText:
Chapter 1: The String Section

Nineteen years before she decided to die, Nora Seed sat in the warmth of the school music room, watching the afternoon sun melt through the large window.

Chapter 2: The Possibility of Nora

Between life and death there is a library, and within that library, the shelves go on forever. Every book provides a chance to try another life you could have lived.

Chapter 3: The Root Cause

Nora walked between the shelves. Each book had her name on the spine. Each was a life she had not lived. Mrs. Elm, the librarian, watched from her desk, waiting for Nora to choose.

title: The God of Small Things
author: Arundhati Roy
description: The Booker Prize-winning debut novel set in Kerala. A story about forbidden love, the caste system, and the lasting impact of a tragic childhood.
preview: May in Ayemenem is a hot, brooding month. The days are long and humid.
fullText:
Chapter 1: Paradise Pickles & Preserves

May in Ayemenem is a hot, brooding month. The days are long and humid. The river shrinks and black crows gorge on bright mangoes in still, dustgreen trees. Red bananas ripen. Jackfruits burst.

Estha and Rahel were twins who arrived in the world twenty minutes apart. They shared a womb, a birthday, the childhood feeling of doubleness. They also shared the loss of what they loved most in the world.

Chapter 2: Pappachi's Moth

The history of the family, like the history of the nation, unspooled backwards. Pappachi was the original sin. His wife Mammachi played the violin and made excellent jam. Their daughter Ammu married the wrong man.

title: A Fine Balance
author: Rohinton Mistry
description: Set in 1970s India during the Emergency, this masterwork follows four unlikely people whose lives become entwined. A heartbreaking portrait of survival, dignity, and human connection.
preview: The morning express bloated with passengers slowed to a crawl, then lurched to a stop.
fullText:
Part I: 1975

The morning express bloated with passengers slowed to a crawl, then lurched to a stop, as though the redness of the signal was painful to it. The train's shadow fell across the earth near a track-side hut where a barefoot boy sat in the dust.

Maneck Kohlah looked out at the parched landscape and at the boy. The boy looked back without curiosity. A cow wandered across the railway track. In the distance, the sun-baked hills offered no shade.

Part II: City by the Sea

Dina Dalal had been a widow for nearly three years when the tailor Ishvar and his nephew Omprakash came to her flat. She was forty-one years old and determined to remain independent of her brother Nusswan.

title: The White Tiger
author: Aravind Adiga
description: The Man Booker Prize-winning novel. The story of Balram Halwai, a village boy who claws his way from poverty to become an entrepreneur in India's booming economy.
preview: My name is Balram Halwai. I was born in the darkness, and I have made my way to the light.
fullText:
The First Night

For the Desk of His Excellency Wen Jiabao, The Premier's Office, Beijing, China.

Mr. Premier,

My name is Balram Halwai, and I am your driver.

I became what I am today at the age of twelve, on a frigid overcast day in the winter of 1975. The moment that changed everything happened in an alley near a frozen creek.

The Second Night

My father's name was Vikram Halwai. We were from Laxmangarh, a small village in the Gaya district of Bihar — one of the darkest corners of India. My father drove a rickshaw. In the city of Dhanbad, he died coughing blood.

title: Gitanjali
author: Rabindranath Tagore
description: The Nobel Prize-winning collection of devotional songs and poems by India's greatest poet. A deeply spiritual work that remains a cornerstone of Indian literature.
preview: Thou hast made me endless, such is thy pleasure. This frail vessel thou emptiest again and again.
fullText:
Song 1

Thou hast made me endless, such is thy pleasure. This frail vessel thou emptiest again and again, and fillest it ever with fresh life.

This little flute of a reed thou hast carried over hills and dales, and hast breathed through it melodies eternally new.

At the immortal touch of thy hands my little heart loses its limits in joy and gives birth to utterance ineffable.

Song 2

When thou commandest me to sing it seems that my heart would break with pride; and I look to thy face, and tears come to my eyes.

All that is harsh and dissonant in my life melts into one sweet harmony — and my adoration spreads wings like a glad bird on its flight across the sea.

Song 3

I know not how thou singest, my master! I ever listen in silent amazement. The light of thy music illumines the world. The life breath of thy music runs from sky to sky.

title: Train to Pakistan
author: Khushwant Singh
description: Set during the Partition of India in 1947, this powerful novel depicts the violence and tragedy that tore apart the subcontinent through the lives of villagers in the border village of Mano Majra.
preview: The summer of 1947 was not like other Indian summers. Even the weather had a different feel in India that year.
fullText:
Part I: Dacoity

The summer of 1947 was not like other Indian summers. Even the weather had a different feel in India that year. There seemed to be a peculiar excitement in the air; not the hot, oppressive, moisture-laden excitement of the monsoon, but the kind that precedes thunderstorms.

The fact is that the summer of 1947 was the last of the British rule. The British Indian empire was breaking up. A new nation called Pakistan was to be born on the 14th of August at the stroke of midnight.

Mano Majra is a tiny place. It has only three brick buildings — one of which is the home of the moneylender Ram Lal. All the other houses are made of mud.

Part II: Mano Majra

Jugga — Jugga Singh — was the local badmash. His father and grandfather had been badmashes before him. The police kept a record of his movements.

title: 2 States
author: Chetan Bhagat
description: The funny and touching story of Krish and Ananya — a Punjabi boy and a Tamil girl who fall in love at IIM Ahmedabad and must convince their very different families to let them marry.
preview: I am a Punjabi. Ananya is Tamil. We fell in love.
fullText:
Chapter 1

I am a Punjabi. Ananya is Tamil. We fell in love. For those who don't know, these two communities are like polar opposites in India. The only thing they share is a taste for oil in their food.

I met Ananya at IIM Ahmedabad. We were in the same section, we sat next to each other in class, and three weeks into the course we were in love.

Chapter 2

Her family was everything mine was not. They ate rice. We ate roti. They spoke Tamil. We spoke Punjabi. Their home smelled of filter coffee. Ours of lassi. But somehow, we had found each other and decided to spend our lives together. We just had to convince two families first.

title: Five Point Someone
author: Chetan Bhagat
description: The bestselling story of three friends at IIT Delhi who fall behind in grades and discover that life and love are about more than just a grade point average.
preview: What not to do at IIT. This is a story of three students who did everything wrong.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Hello, my name is Hari Kumar and I just screwed up the most important examinations of my life.

I met my two best friends, Ryan and Alok, in the very first week at IIT Delhi. Ryan Oberoi — the guy who questions everything. Alok Gupta — the guy who worries about everything. And me, Hari Kumar — the guy caught in between.

We were assigned the same room in Kumaon hostel. Within hours of meeting, we had decided we would either conquer IIT together or go down together.

Chapter 2

Professor Cherian was the most feared professor in the Mechanical Engineering department. He had a PhD from MIT and hadn't smiled since 1987. He also had a daughter named Neha, which was where my problems really began.

title: Malgudi Days
author: R.K. Narayan
description: A beloved collection of short stories set in the fictional South Indian town of Malgudi, capturing everyday Indian life with wit, warmth, and gentle irony.
preview: Malgudi — a fictional town in South India that feels more real than most real places.
fullText:
An Astrologer's Day

Punctually at midday he opened his bag and spread out his professional equipment, which consisted of a dozen cowrie shells, a square piece of cloth with mystic charts on it, a notebook, and a bundle of palmyra writing.

His forehead was resplendent with sacred ash and vermilion, and his eyes sparkled with a sharp abnormal gleam which his simple clients took to be a prophetic light and felt comforted.

The Blind Dog

The dog was a stray, living on the offal thrown out by the butcher in the narrow lanes of the market. It had no name and moved about without purpose. One afternoon it was sitting in the shade of a shop, its eyes blinking in the glare. A beggar, who had been lying on the road nearby, watched it for a while.

Self-Help

Gopu was in his sixties when he decided to become a writer. He had retired from thirty years of government service and had a pension. He had time. He had opinions. What he did not have was any idea how difficult writing actually was.

title: Godan
author: Munshi Premchand
description: The greatest Hindi novel ever written. The story of Hori, a poor Indian farmer whose lifelong dream is to own a cow. A devastating portrait of rural poverty and unbroken human spirit.
preview: Hori's life revolved around a single dream — to own a cow.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Hori Ram, the peasant, set out for the zamindar's house with a bundle of fresh-cut grass on his head. It was early morning. The sun had not yet risen, but a faint grey light spread over the fields.

Hori's dream was simple. He wanted a cow. Not a buffalo, not a goat — a cow. A real milking cow that he could call his own. In thirty years of farming, he had never owned one.

His wife Dhania called after him: 'Don't forget to bow low to the Rai Sahib. And ask him about the loan. We must pay something before the harvest or the interest will eat us alive.'

Chapter 2

The zamindar Rai Sahib sat in his courtyard on a low wooden seat. He was a large man who moved slowly, as though the weight of his own importance tired him. Around him sat his hangers-on, laughing at his jokes and nodding at his opinions.

title: Nirmala
author: Munshi Premchand
description: A powerful social novel about Nirmala, a young girl forced into a mismatched marriage due to the dowry system. Premchand's searing critique of social injustice against women in colonial India.
preview: Nirmala was only fifteen when her father died. The burden of her marriage fell entirely on her mother's thin shoulders.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Babu Udaybhanu Lal was a lawyer in a small town. He had two daughters and one son. The elder daughter, Nirmala, was fifteen years old. She was beautiful, gentle, and studious.

When Babu Udaybhanu died suddenly, leaving behind debts and very little money, the burden of getting Nirmala married fell on her mother Kalyani Devi.

Finally, an offer came from a middle-aged widower named Totaram who had three children from his first marriage. He was willing to marry Nirmala for a modest dowry.

Chapter 2

Nirmala arrived at her new home like a guest who has come to the wrong address. The house was large and dark. Her stepchildren regarded her with suspicion. Her husband regarded her with something she could not name.

title: Wings of Fire
author: A.P.J. Abdul Kalam
description: The inspiring autobiography of India's most beloved President and scientist. From humble beginnings in Rameswaram to leading India's space and missile programs.
preview: I was born into a middle-class Tamil family in the island town of Rameswaram.
fullText:
Chapter 1: Childhood

I was born into a middle-class Tamil family in the island town of Rameswaram in the erstwhile Madras Presidency. My father, Jainulabdeen, had neither much formal education nor much wealth; despite these disadvantages, he possessed great innate wisdom and a true generosity of spirit.

My mother, Ashiamma, was an ideal helpmate to my father. I do not recall the exact number of people she fed every day, but I am quite certain that far more outsiders ate with us than all the members of our own family put together.

I had three brothers and a sister. I was the youngest child.

Chapter 2: Early Education

The primary school I attended was just down the road from our house. Our teacher, Sivasubramania Iyer, was one of the finest teachers I have known. Although his students were poor, he never made them feel poor. He told us that poverty was not a permanent condition but a transitional stage.

title: The Immortals of Meluha
author: Amish Tripathi
description: Book 1 of the Shiva Trilogy. A fictional retelling of Shiva — not as a god, but as a man who becomes one. A thrilling blend of mythology, history, and adventure set in ancient India.
preview: The Tibetan plateau, 1900 BC. A young tribal chief named Shiva leads his people across the Himalayas into the legendary land of Meluha.
fullText:
Chapter 1: He Arrives — 1900 BC

The Tibetan plateau was harsh and unforgiving. The wind howled across the frozen landscape, cutting through the thickest skins. But Shiva, chief of the Gunas tribe, felt nothing of the cold. He was lost in thought.

He was twenty-one years old. Strong, broad-shouldered, with a wild beard and thick matted hair. A warrior from the moment he could walk. But Shiva was unlike other warriors — he thought before he fought.

'We should go, Shiva,' said Bhadra, his closest companion. 'The Meluha delegation is waiting. They say their land has prosperity and peace beyond anything we know.'

Chapter 2: The Land of the Perfect

Meluha was everything the delegation had promised and more. The roads were straight and clean. The buildings were white and uniform. The people were well-fed and orderly. And everywhere, the presence of the great Ram's laws was felt.

title: Devdas
author: Sarat Chandra Chattopadhyay
description: One of the most celebrated love stories in Indian literature. The tragic tale of Devdas and Paro — a love destroyed by family and society — that has inspired countless Bollywood films.
preview: Devdas and Paro grew up together in the same village on the banks of the river in Bengal.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Devdas and Paro grew up together in the same village on the banks of the river in Bengal. Their childhood love was pure and innocent — the kind that exists before the world teaches you about caste and class and propriety.

Devdas was the son of the wealthy zamindar. Paro was the daughter of a woman who had come from outside. Their love was not permitted by society.

Chapter 2

When Devdas left for Calcutta to study, he promised Paro he would come back and marry her. She believed him with all her heart. But when he returned, he was changed. He could not fight his family. He could not stand up for their love. And so he walked away — and found the bottle instead.

Chapter 3

In Calcutta, Devdas met Chandramukhi. She was a courtesan, but she loved him honestly — more honestly than his circumstances had ever allowed him to love Paro. He could not see this. He was too lost in the maze of his own grief.

title: Midnight's Children
author: Salman Rushdie
description: The Booker of Bookers Prize winner. Born at the exact moment of India's independence, Saleem Sinai discovers he has telepathic powers and is mysteriously linked to all other children born at midnight.
preview: I, Saleem Sinai, later variously called Snotnose, Stainface, Baldy, Sniffer, had my nativity determined by time and place.
fullText:
Chapter 1: The Perforated Sheet

I was born in the city of Bombay... once upon a time. No, that will not do, there is too much of once upon a time in it.

I, Saleem Sinai, later variously called Snotnose, Stainface, Baldy, Sniffer, Buddha and even Piece-of-the-Moon, had my nativity determined by time and place. I was born on the stroke of midnight on August 15, 1947 — at the precise instant of India's independence.

I have been a swallower of lives; and to know me, just the one of me, you'll have to swallow the lot as well.

Chapter 2: Mercurochrome

My grandfather Aadam Aziz had a nose to reckon with — large, outstanding, a proboscis of wonderful and overpowering power. A nose that was an inheritance, a destiny, a curse.

title: The Namesake
author: Jhumpa Lahiri
description: A moving story of the Ganguli family's immigration from Calcutta to America and their son Gogol's struggle to reconcile two worlds, two cultures, and ultimately himself.
preview: On a sticky August evening two weeks before her due date, Ashima Ganguli stands in the kitchen of a Central Square apartment.
fullText:
Part One

On a sticky August evening two weeks before her due date, Ashima Ganguli stands in the kitchen of a Central Square apartment, combining Rice Krispies and Planters peanuts and chopped red onion in a bowl. She adds salt, lemon juice, thin slices of green chili pepper.

She is twenty-seven years old, seven thousand miles from the city where she was born. She is waiting for her husband, Ashoke, to come home from the university where he is completing his dissertation.

The baby drops steadily on her bladder. She does not feel at home in this country — not yet, perhaps not ever.

Part Two

Gogol Ganguli hated his name. He had always hated it. Not just because it sounded strange to American ears, not just because his classmates called him 'Goggles' — but because of the story behind it.

title: Palace of Illusions
author: Chitra Banerjee Divakaruni
description: A stunning retelling of the Mahabharata from the perspective of Draupadi. One woman's voice gives new life to the ancient epic, exploring ambition, love, war, and destiny.
preview: I was born from fire. There was an actual fire, a consuming column of flame that erupted from the centre of the sacrificial pit.
fullText:
Chapter 1: The Birth

I was born from fire.

I don't say this as metaphor. There was an actual fire, a consuming column of flame that erupted from the centre of the sacrificial pit, making the priests stumble back in shock. And out of it I stepped, already a young woman, already beautiful, already dressed in a sari the color of flame.

My father had asked for a son. He was king of Panchaal, a proud and powerful king, but he had suffered a humiliation that festered in his heart. He wanted revenge, and for revenge he needed a son. Instead, he got me.

Chapter 2: The Palace of Lac

The Pandava brothers came to Panchaal for the great swayamvar. I watched them from behind the curtain. All five of them — but my eyes kept returning to the third one, the archer Arjun.

title: Guide
author: R.K. Narayan
description: The Sahitya Akademi Award-winning novel about Raju, a tour guide who becomes a spiritual leader by accident. A profound meditation on destiny, transformation, and the nature of faith.
preview: The moment Raju had set eyes on him, he had decided to call him Railway Raju.
fullText:
Chapter 1

The moment Raju had set eyes on him, he had decided to call him 'Railway Raju'.

Raju was sitting on a stone slab outside the old shrine, watching the arrival of the villagers. The shrine had been deserted for years. Now the villagers had returned and were rebuilding their homes.

'Swami, our village has been rebuilt. We need your blessings.' The man bowed low.

Raju thought quickly. He had arrived here only three days ago, after walking for hours through the jungle, half-starved. He had found this deserted shrine and slept in it. And now the villagers had mistaken him for a holy man.

Chapter 2

Rosie had been a dancer all her life — a Devadasi tradition, her mother always said. But Rosie wanted to be more than tradition. She wanted a life. When she met Raju the guide, she thought she had found one.

title: The Discovery of India
author: Jawaharlal Nehru
description: Written by India's first Prime Minister while imprisoned by the British, this masterwork traces India's history from ancient civilizations to the independence movement.
preview: I was sitting alone in Ahmadnagar Fort when I began writing this book.
fullText:
Preface

I was sitting alone in Ahmadnagar Fort when I began writing this book. Months had passed since I had been arrested and brought to this old Moghul fort.

Chapter 1: The Quest

India is a discovery for me. Though I have lived in this country for fifty-odd years, I have not found it yet. The diversity of her people, the complexity of her history, the richness of her culture — all this unfolds layer upon layer, never yielding its final secret.

India is a geographical and economic entity, a cultural unity amidst diversity, a bundle of contradictions held together by strong but invisible threads.

Chapter 2: The Indus Valley Civilisation

Archaeology has given us glimpses of a civilization that flourished five thousand years ago in the Indus valley — a civilization as advanced in many ways as those of Egypt and Mesopotamia.

title: Crime and Punishment
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
description: A young student commits a terrible crime believing himself above conventional morality, then suffers the psychological torment of guilt. A landmark of world literature.
preview: On an exceptionally hot evening early in July a young man came out of the garret in which he lodged.
fullText:
Part I, Chapter 1

On an exceptionally hot evening early in July a young man came out of the garret in which he lodged in S. Place and walked slowly, as though in hesitation, towards K. Bridge.

He had successfully avoided meeting his landlady on the staircase. His garret was under the roof of a high, five-storied house and was more like a cupboard than a room.

He was hopelessly in debt to his landlady, and was afraid of meeting her. Not because he was cowardly — quite the contrary; but for some time past he had been in an overstrained irritable condition, verging on hypochondria.

Part I, Chapter 2

He was remarkably handsome, above the average in height, slim, well-built, with beautiful dark eyes and dark brown hair. Soon he sank into deep thought, or more accurately speaking into a sort of unconsciousness.

title: One Hundred Years of Solitude
author: Gabriel García Márquez
description: The Nobel Prize-winning masterpiece of magical realism. The multi-generational story of the Buendía family in the fictional town of Macondo.
preview: Many years later, as he faced the firing squad, Colonel Aureliano Buendía was to remember that distant afternoon when his father took him to discover ice.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Many years later, as he faced the firing squad, Colonel Aureliano Buendía was to remember that distant afternoon when his father took him to discover ice. At that time Macondo was a village of twenty adobe houses, built on the bank of a river of clear water that ran along a bed of polished stones, which were white and enormous, like prehistoric eggs.

The world was so recent that many things lacked names, and in order to indicate them it was necessary to point.

Chapter 2

José Arcadio Buendía spent many long hours building a room for Melquíades, who slept three whole months in it. He moved in the almanac and the magnifying glass, the old man's way of seeing the world through miniature fire.

title: Anna Karenina
author: Leo Tolstoy
description: Tolstoy's masterpiece about a married aristocrat who abandons her family for a passionate affair with Count Vronsky — with tragic consequences.
preview: Happy families are all alike; every unhappy family is unhappy in its own way.
fullText:
Part One, Chapter 1

Happy families are all alike; every unhappy family is unhappy in its own way.

Everything was in confusion in the Oblonskys' house. The wife had discovered that the husband was carrying on an intrigue with a French girl, who had been a governess in their family, and she had announced to her husband that she could not go on living in the same house with him.

This position of affairs had now lasted three days, and not only the husband and wife themselves, but all the members of their family and household, were painfully conscious of it.

Part One, Chapter 2

Princess Shcherbatsky was eighteen years old and had been out in society for one winter already. Kitty had two suitors — Vronsky and Levin.

title: The Kite Runner
author: Khaled Hosseini
description: A moving story of friendship, betrayal, and redemption set against Afghanistan from the fall of the monarchy to the Taliban. An unforgettable tale of guilt and loyalty.
preview: I thought about Hassan's harelip. I thought about the winter of 1975, coming up on us now like a pair of eyes in the dark.
fullText:
Chapter 1

I became what I am today at the age of twelve, on a frigid overcast day in the winter of 1975. I remember the precise moment, crouching behind a crumbling mud wall, peeking into the alley near the frozen creek. That was a long time ago, but it's wrong what they say about the past, I've learned, about how you can bury it. Because the past claws its way out.

Chapter 2

When we were children, Hassan and I used to climb the poplar trees in the driveway of my father's house and annoy our neighbors by reflecting sunlight into their homes with a shard of mirror.

Hassan was the son of my father's Hazara servant Ali. But Hassan and I fed from the same breast. We toddled on the same lawns. And in the same pale blue crib.

title: The Old Man and the Sea
author: Ernest Hemingway
description: The Pulitzer Prize-winning story of an aging Cuban fisherman who struggles with a giant marlin far out in the Gulf Stream. A profound meditation on perseverance and the human spirit.
preview: He was an old man who fished alone in a skiff in the Gulf Stream and he had gone eighty-four days now without taking a fish.
fullText:
He was an old man who fished alone in a skiff in the Gulf Stream and he had gone eighty-four days now without taking a fish. In the first forty days a boy had been with him. But after forty days without a fish the boy's parents had told him that the old man was now definitely and finally salao, which is the worst form of unlucky.

It made the boy sad to see the old man come in each day with his skiff empty and he always went down to help him carry either the coiled lines or the gaff and harpoon and the sail that was furled around the mast.

The sail was patched with flour sacks and, furled, it looked like the flag of permanent defeat.

The old man was thin and gaunt with deep wrinkles in the back of his neck. His hands had the deep-creased scars from handling heavy fish on the cords. But none of these scars were fresh. They were as old as erosions in a fishless desert.

title: Ikigai
author: Héctor García & Francesc Miralles
description: The Japanese secret to a long and happy life. Explore the concept of ikigai — your reason for being — and how it can give your life purpose, passion, and extraordinary longevity.
preview: What is your reason for getting up in the morning? What gives your life meaning?
fullText:
Introduction

What is your reason for getting up in the morning? What gives your life meaning? The Japanese have a concept for this that has no direct translation — they call it ikigai.

Chapter 1: Ikigai — The Art of Staying Young While Growing Old

Only staying active will make you want to live a hundred years. This Japanese proverb captures something essential about the island of Okinawa, where more centenarians live than anywhere else on earth.

What is their secret? The researchers who have studied them point to diet, social bonds, and something else — a clear sense of purpose in life. They all have an ikigai.

Chapter 2: Finding Flow in Everything

Flow is the state of complete immersion in an activity. Csikszentmihalyi described it as the state in which people are so involved in an activity that nothing else seems to matter. In Okinawa, they find flow in everything — in the garden, in the market, in the dance.

title: Interpreter of Maladies
author: Jhumpa Lahiri
description: The Pulitzer Prize-winning debut collection exploring the lives of Indian and Indian-American characters navigating between two cultures, belonging fully to neither.
preview: At the tea stall Mr. and Mrs. Das and their three children sat down to eat, while Mr. Kapasi waited in the car.
fullText:
Interpreter of Maladies

At the tea stall Mr. and Mrs. Das and their three children sat down to eat, while Mr. Kapasi waited in the car. He observed them — a family of five, American tourists.

Mr. Kapasi had offered himself as their driver for the day. They were headed to the Sun Temple at Konarak.

'Are you the interpreter of maladies?' Mrs. Das asked him once they were back in the car.

'In a way,' he said. 'I interpret what the patients say in Gujarati to the doctor who only speaks Bengali and English.'

A Real Durwan

Boori Ma swept the stairwell every morning — the same stairwell she had been sweeping for years, since she had nowhere else to go. She was the building's durwan, its watchwoman, even though she owned nothing.

title: The Secret
author: Rhonda Byrne
description: The worldwide phenomenon based on the Law of Attraction — the claim that positive thinking can create positive results in health, wealth, and happiness.
preview: The Great Secret of Life is the law of attraction.
fullText:
Chapter 1: The Secret Revealed

The Great Secret of Life is the law of attraction.

The law of attraction says like attracts like, so when you think a thought, you are also attracting like thoughts to you. Thoughts are magnetic, and thoughts have a frequency.

Everything that has been invented and created throughout the history of humankind began with one thought. From that one thought a way was made, and it manifested from the invisible into the visible.

Chapter 2: The Secret Made Simple

Thoughts become things. If you see it in your mind, you will hold it in your hand. This is not a metaphor — it is the literal operating principle of the universe.

Chapter 3: How to Use the Secret

There are three steps to using the Secret: Ask, Believe, Receive. These steps have been taught by every great teacher and every great tradition in every civilization.

title: Half Girlfriend
author: Chetan Bhagat
description: Madhav Jha from Bihar falls for Riya Somani, a Delhi girl who agrees to be his 'half girlfriend' — not fully committed, but not entirely distant either.
preview: My name is Madhav Jha. I come from Dumraon, a small town in Bihar.
fullText:
Chapter 1

My name is Madhav Jha. I come from Dumraon, a small town in Bihar. I went to St. Stephens College, Delhi — one of the most prestigious colleges in India. Getting there was a miracle. Staying there was a daily struggle.

I couldn't speak English well. The Delhi boys and girls laughed at my accent. They laughed at my clothes. They laughed at everything about me.

But then I met her. Riya Somani. And everything changed. Or maybe nothing changed. Maybe that was the problem.

Chapter 2

Riya Somani was the kind of girl Bihar boys dream about. She had been to Vasant Valley School, then to Stephen's. Her Hindi was worse than my English but she played basketball better than anyone I had ever seen.

title: The Remains of the Day
author: Kazuo Ishiguro
description: The Booker Prize-winning novel about an English butler who reflects on his life of service, slowly realizing the personal cost of absolute professionalism.
preview: It seems increasingly likely that I really will undertake the expedition that has been preoccupying my imagination now for some days.
fullText:
Day One — Evening: Salisbury

It seems increasingly likely that I really will undertake the expedition that has been preoccupying my imagination now for some days. An expedition, I should say, which I will undertake alone, in the employer's Ford, to the West Country.

For a number of years now, I have been contented with the routine of life at Darlington Hall. But I confess there have been moments when certain questions have occurred to me concerning the manner in which I have directed my life.

The fact is, I have rarely thought about what might have been had I not devoted myself so completely to the service of a great household.

Day Two — Morning: Salisbury

I find that I have woken up rather early. It is not yet seven o'clock and the inn is still very quiet. I have been lying in bed going over yesterday's events in my mind.

title: Raag Darbari
author: Shrilal Shukla
description: The Sahitya Akademi Award-winning satirical Hindi novel that brilliantly skewers post-Independence Indian village politics, bureaucracy, and the gap between idealism and ground reality.
preview: The truck was loaded with sugar cane. Ranganath was sitting on top of the load, looking at the road ahead.
fullText:
Chapter 1

The truck was loaded with sugar cane. Ranganath was sitting on top of the load, looking at the road ahead.

He was coming to Shivpalganj to visit his maternal uncle, Vaidyaji. Ranganath was an M.A. in Sociology from the city. He had grown up reading about India — about its poverty, its corruption, its great untapped potential. Now he was about to see it first-hand.

As the truck entered the village, a group of men were sitting outside a shop, arguing. They had been arguing, it seemed, for hours. About what? Everything. Nothing. This was Shivpalganj.

Chapter 2

Vaidyaji ran the local college, the local cooperative, and through various intermediaries, the local police station. He was a man of influence. Not formally powerful — formally he held no office — but informally omnipotent.

title: A Suitable Boy
author: Vikram Seth
description: One of the longest novels in the English language. Set in 1950s India, it follows Lata Mehra as her mother searches for a suitable husband for her, weaving together four families against newly independent India.
preview: 'You too will marry a boy I choose,' said Mrs. Rupa Mehra to her younger daughter.
fullText:
Part 1

'You too will marry a boy I choose,' said Mrs. Rupa Mehra to her younger daughter.

Lata only smiled. She was used to her mother's assumptions, her certainties, her attempts to define the world. Mrs. Mehra was a practical woman. She believed that love before marriage was a luxury that India could not yet afford.

The year was 1951. India had been independent for four years. The first general election was approaching. And in the household of Mrs. Mehra, the most pressing question was not who would govern India, but who would marry Lata.

Part 2

Lata Mehra, nineteen years old, was a student at Brahmpur University. She was studying English literature. She had read Jane Austen and Hardy and was inclined to think that the great love of her life lay somewhere ahead of her, not yet encountered.

title: The Secret Garden
author: Frances Hodgson Burnett
description: A beloved classic about a neglected orphan girl named Mary who discovers a hidden, locked garden and finds healing, friendship, and the magic of new life in its forgotten soil.
preview: When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle, everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen.
fullText:
Chapter 1: There Is No One Left

When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle, everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too.

She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another.

Her father had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties. So the little girl had been kept out of the way.

Chapter 2: Mistress Mary Quite Contrary

At first, each day which passed at Misselthwaite Manor was exactly like the others. Every morning she awoke in her tapestried room and stared at the ceiling — the same ceiling, the same walls, the same cold fire.

title: Chokher Bali
author: Rabindranath Tagore
description: Tagore's sensational novel about a young widow, Binodini, whose passionate nature clashes with the conservative society around her. A bold exploration of desire, friendship, and repressed lives.
preview: Binodini was a widow at nineteen. She had beauty, intelligence, and passion.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Binodini was a widow at nineteen.

She had beauty, intelligence, and passion — all the qualities that her society considered dangerous in a woman without a husband. She had been sent to live with her in-laws after her husband's early death, and there she was expected to fade quietly into the background of life.

But Binodini refused to fade.

When she came to visit her friend Ashalata — Asha, they called her — Asha's young husband Mahendra noticed her immediately. How could he not? There was something about Binodini that demanded to be noticed.

Chapter 2

Mahendra was a medical student, a rational man who had always believed he was immune to the kind of passion that ruined other men. He had married Asha for her gentleness. He had not expected Binodini.

title: The Monk Who Sold His Ferrari
author: Robin Sharma
description: A fable about a successful lawyer who suffers a heart attack, gives up everything, and travels to India to find wisdom. He returns to share seven timeless life lessons.
preview: Julian Mantle was a great lawyer. He was also, deep down, a profoundly unhappy man.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Julian Mantle was a great lawyer. He was also, deep down, a profoundly unhappy man.

He had a Ferrari, a mansion, a private jet, and a life that looked wonderful from the outside. But inside, Julian was falling apart. He worked eighteen-hour days, had no time for family or friends, and had long since forgotten what it felt like to laugh.

On a Tuesday morning in October, Julian collapsed in the middle of a court hearing. Heart attack. He was fifty-three years old.

When he recovered, he did something nobody expected. He sold everything — the Ferrari, the mansion, all of it — and flew to India.

Chapter 2: The Mysterious Visitor

Julian returned three years later, transformed. He was lean, serene, and full of a quiet joy that made him almost unrecognisable. He came to see me — his old law partner — and he had a story to tell.

title: Pather Panchali
author: Bibhutibhushan Bandyopadhyay
description: The masterpiece that inspired Satyajit Ray's legendary film trilogy. The story of Apu and Durga — a beautiful, melancholy portrait of a poor Bengali family and childhood in rural India.
preview: The village of Nishchindipur lay among orchards and bamboo groves. Durga and her little brother Apu grew up in its dust and sunlight.
fullText:
Chapter 1

The village of Nishchindipur lay among orchards and bamboo groves on the edge of the world, or so it seemed to the two children who grew up there.

Durga was the elder. She was twelve — wild-haired, quick-footed, always in motion. Her brother Apu was seven, small for his age, with enormous wondering eyes that took in everything.

Their father, Horihor, was a priest and dreamer. He was always about to write a play that would make them all famous and wealthy. He never did. Their mother Shorbojoya kept the family alive through sheer stubbornness.

Chapter 2

Durga had a habit of stealing fruit from the neighbour's orchard. Not from hunger, exactly — though there was often not enough food — but from a kind of wild joy in the doing of it. Apu followed her everywhere, half-terrified, wholly devoted.

title: Brave New World
author: Aldous Huxley
description: A prophetic vision of a future world where citizens are engineered from birth and happiness is enforced through pleasure and conditioning. One of the great novels of the 20th century.
preview: A squat grey building of only thirty-four stories. Over the main entrance the words, CENTRAL LONDON HATCHERY AND CONDITIONING CENTRE.
fullText:
Chapter 1

A squat grey building of only thirty-four stories. Over the main entrance the words, CENTRAL LONDON HATCHERY AND CONDITIONING CENTRE, and, in a shield, the World State's motto, COMMUNITY, IDENTITY, STABILITY.

The enormous room on the ground floor faced towards the north. Cold for all the summer beyond the panes, for all the tropical heat of the room itself, a harsh thin light glared through the windows, hungrily seeking some draped lay figure.

Chapter 2

Mr. Foster was left in the Fertilizing Room. The D.H.C. and his students stepped into the nearest lift and were carried up to the fifth floor. INFANT NURSERIES. NEO-PAVLOVIAN CONDITIONING ROOMS, announced the notice board.

title: The Jungle Book
author: Rudyard Kipling
description: The timeless tales of Mowgli, the boy raised by wolves in the jungles of India. Stories set in India that have delighted children and adults worldwide for over a century.
preview: It was seven o'clock of a very warm evening in the Seeonee hills when Father Wolf woke up from his day's rest.
fullText:
Mowgli's Brothers

It was seven o'clock of a very warm evening in the Seeonee hills when Father Wolf woke up from his day's rest, scratched himself, yawned, and spread out his paws one after the other to get rid of the sleepy feeling in their tips.

Mother Wolf lay with her big grey nose dropped across her four tumbling, squealing cubs, and the moon shone into the mouth of the cave where they all lived.

'Augrh!' said Father Wolf. 'It is time to hunt again.'

Kaa's Hunting

His spots shall show,
His hide shall glow,
His jaws shall close on the prey;
He shall track the trail
Through the forest pale,
As swiftly as shadows may.

title: Think and Grow Rich
author: Napoleon Hill
description: One of the best-selling books of all time. Napoleon Hill's timeless classic on the principles of success, drawn from interviews with the most successful people of the 20th century.
preview: In every chapter of this book, mention has been made of the money-making secret which has made fortunes for hundreds of exceedingly wealthy men.
fullText:
Introduction

In every chapter of this book, mention has been made of the money-making secret which has made fortunes for hundreds of exceedingly wealthy men whom I have carefully analysed over a long period of years.

Chapter 1: Thoughts Are Things

Truly, 'thoughts are things,' and powerful things at that, when they are mixed with definiteness of purpose, persistence, and a burning desire for their translation into riches, or other material objects.

Edwin C. Barnes discovered how true it is that men really do think and grow rich. When he decided to become the business associate of Thomas Edison, he was no better off than thousands of others who had made the same decision.

Chapter 2: Desire — The Starting Point of All Achievement

The starting point of all achievement is desire. Keep this constantly in mind. Weak desire brings weak results, just as a small amount of fire makes a small amount of heat.

title: The Power of Now
author: Eckhart Tolle
description: A guide to spiritual enlightenment and living in the present moment. Tolle shares his transformation from despair to awakening and shows how to access true happiness.
preview: I have little use for the past and rarely think about it. The future, too, hardly exists for me.
fullText:
Chapter 1: You Are Not Your Mind

I have little use for the past and rarely think about it. The future, too, hardly exists for me. The Power of Now deals with the eternal present — the only moment that actually exists.

The greatest obstacle to experiencing this reality is identification with your mind, which causes thought to become compulsive.

Chapter 2: Consciousness: The Way Out of Pain

Creating Pain: There is a direct link between the inability to be present and the illusion of past and future. The mind is essentially a survival machine. Attack and defense against other minds, gathering, storing, and analyzing information — this is what it does.

Chapter 3: Moving Deeply into the Now

The present moment is where life is. Do not be concerned with how your future will unfold. Focus completely on what you are doing right now.

title: The Subtle Art of Not Giving a F*ck
author: Mark Manson
description: A counterintuitive guide to living a good life. Manson argues that improving our lives hinges on accepting our limitations and choosing carefully what to care about.
preview: Charles Bukowski was a writer of poor taste and low ambition. He had a great deal of talent but wasted most of it.
fullText:
Chapter 1: Don't Try

Charles Bukowski was a writer of poor taste and low ambition who was once described as a lowly janitor's assistant who worked in a dog food factory.

He was also an alcoholic, a womaniser, and a gambler. But for all his bravado and posturing, Bukowski was also a man who cared deeply about one thing: writing.

Somewhere, beyond all the failure and rejection, he clung to the idea that he had something meaningful to say. And that was enough to keep him going.

Chapter 2: Happiness Is a Problem

The belief that there is something inherently wrong with feeling unhappy is unhealthy. There is value in suffering. It signals that something is wrong and needs to be changed.

title: Shoe Dog
author: Phil Knight
description: The memoir from Nike's founder Phil Knight — a candid and riveting account of how he and a band of misfits created one of the world's most iconic brands against all odds.
preview: In 1962, fresh out of business school, I convinced my father to lend me fifty dollars to visit Japan.
fullText:
1962

In 1962, fresh out of business school, I convinced my father to lend me fifty dollars to visit Japan.

I was twenty-four years old. I had a newly minted MBA from Stanford Business School, and I thought I was about to go on the adventure of my life. I didn't know yet that the adventure would last forty years.

I landed in Tokyo. I had a crazy idea: to sell Japanese running shoes in America. I had no company, no money, no manufacturing experience. What I had was a conviction — that American athletes deserved better shoes.

1963

I had promised my father I would return after a year and get a 'real job.' Instead I found myself sitting across from the chairman of Onitsuka Tiger, trying to explain my imaginary company, Blue Ribbon Sports.

title: Educated
author: Tara Westover
description: A memoir about a young woman who never went to school until age seventeen, and then went on to earn a PhD from Cambridge. A remarkable story of self-invention.
preview: I'm standing on the mountain of memories. Memory is fallible and it is all I have.
fullText:
Chapter 1

I'm standing on the mountain of memories. Memory is fallible and it is all I have.

I grew up in the mountains of Idaho. My father was a survivalist who believed the government was his enemy. My mother was a healer who used herbs and essential oils. They had seven children. I was the youngest.

We did not go to school. My father said school was government brainwashing. My mother taught us, when she taught us at all. Mostly I learned from watching my brothers.

Chapter 2

The first time I entered a classroom I was seventeen years old. I sat in a desk that was exactly like the desks I had read about in books, surrounded by students who talked about things I had never heard of — things they assumed everyone knew.

title: The Art of War
author: Sun Tzu
description: The ancient Chinese military treatise that has been applied to business, sports, and life for over 2,500 years. Timeless wisdom on strategy, conflict, and leadership.
preview: The art of war is of vital importance to the State. It is a matter of life and death, a road either to safety or to ruin.
fullText:
Chapter 1: Laying Plans

The art of war is of vital importance to the State. It is a matter of life and death, a road either to safety or to ruin. Hence it is a subject of inquiry which can on no account be neglected.

The art of war, then, is governed by five constant factors, to be taken into account in one's deliberations, when seeking to determine the conditions obtaining in the field. These are: The Moral Law; Heaven; Earth; The Commander; Method and discipline.

Chapter 2: Waging War

In the operations of war, where there are in the field a thousand swift chariots, as many heavy chariots, and a hundred thousand mail-clad soldiers, with provisions enough to carry them a thousand li, the expenditure at home and at the front will be a thousand ounces of silver per day.

title: Norwegian Wood
author: Haruki Murakami
description: A nostalgic story of loss and sexuality set in Tokyo in the late 1960s. A young man is drawn to two very different women as he struggles with grief and his own sense of self.
preview: I was thirty-seven then, strapped in my seat as the Narita-bound 747 prepared to land.
fullText:
Chapter 1

I was thirty-seven then, strapped in my seat as the Narita-bound 747 prepared to land. I had been in Europe and was returning to Japan. The Beatles' Norwegian Wood came on the cabin audio system, and the melody made me sad in a way I couldn't explain.

That song was from 1969. I was nineteen that year. Two people I loved both died that year — one by choice, one by chance. And I, Toru Watanabe, drifted between them, unable to save either.

Chapter 2

Naoko was Kizuki's girlfriend. Kizuki was my best friend. On a November day of his eighteenth year, without any warning or reason, Kizuki chose to end his life in his own garage.

title: Kafka on the Shore
author: Haruki Murakami
description: A metaphysical masterpiece weaving two parallel narratives — a runaway teenager and an old man who can talk to cats — into a profound meditation on fate, identity, and memory.
preview: On my fifteenth birthday, I'll run away from home and head for Tokyo.
fullText:
Chapter 1

On my fifteenth birthday, I'll run away from home and head for Tokyo. That's the plan.

My name, for now, is Kafka Tamura. Kafka is my self-chosen name. My father gave me a different name, one I dislike. My father also gave me a prophecy: that I would one day kill him and sleep with my mother and sister.

I am doing everything in my power to prevent this from coming true.

Chapter 2

Nakata was not a clever man. He knew this, accepted it, and built his life accordingly.

When he was young, he had been an ordinary student. Then, during the war, something had happened — something in the mountains he had been evacuated to. After that he could no longer read, could no longer learn. But he could talk to cats.

title: The Stranger
author: Albert Camus
description: The foundational existentialist novel. Meursault, an emotionally detached man in French Algeria, commits a murder and faces his consequeces with philosophical indifference.
preview: Mother died today. Or maybe yesterday; I can't be sure.
fullText:
Part One

Mother died today. Or maybe yesterday; I can't be sure. The telegram from the Home says: YOUR MOTHER PASSED AWAY. FUNERAL TOMORROW. DEEP SYMPATHY. Which leaves the matter doubtful; it could have been yesterday.

The Home for Aged Persons is at Marengo, some fifty miles from Algiers. I'll take the two o'clock bus and get there in the afternoon. That way I can be back in Algiers tomorrow night.

Part Two

I think I understood that I had been given a verdict in a sentence that was final. I remember that the judge asked me if I had anything to say. I said no. And the court rose, and then everyone filed out, and I was left alone.

title: The Count of Monte Cristo
author: Alexandre Dumas
description: A sweeping tale of wrongful imprisonment, escape, and elaborate revenge. Edmond Dantès, falsely imprisoned, becomes the mysterious Count of Monte Cristo and exacts meticulous vengeance on those who betrayed him.
preview: On the 24th of February, 1815, the lookout at Notre-Dame de la Garde signalled the arrival of the three-master Pharaon from Smyrna.
fullText:
Chapter 1: Marseilles — The Arrival

On the 24th of February, 1815, the lookout at Notre-Dame de la Garde signalled the arrival of the three-master Pharaon from Smyrna, Trieste, and Naples.

As usual, a pilot came out immediately from Fort Saint-Jean, and the vessel was brought to anchor off the Pillon to be loaded.

As always, the shore was covered with spectators. Edmond Dantès, nineteen years old, was at the helm. He was a good-looking young man with the frank open countenance of a man accustomed to a life of adventure.

Chapter 2: Father and Son

Mercédès loved Edmond, and Edmond loved Mercédès. But there were other men who loved Mercédès — and they were prepared to do anything to separate her from Dantès.

title: Gone with the Wind
author: Margaret Mitchell
description: The epic love story of Scarlett O'Hara and Rhett Butler set against the backdrop of the American Civil War and Reconstruction — a Pulitzer Prize-winning tale of survival and passion.
preview: Scarlett O'Hara was not beautiful, but men seldom realised it when caught by her charm.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Scarlett O'Hara was not beautiful, but men seldom realised it when caught by her charm as the Tarleton twins were. In her face were too sharply blended the delicate features of her mother, a Coast aristocrat of French descent, and the heavy ones of her florid Irish father.

But it was an arresting face, pointed of chin, square of jaw. Her eyes were pale green without a touch of hazel, starred with bristly black lashes and slightly tilted at the ends.

Chapter 2

The war came and went and took everything: the O'Hara wealth, the world Scarlett had been raised to inhabit, the men she had loved. But Scarlett O'Hara was made of different material than most of the Georgia belles.

title: The Brothers Karamazov
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
description: Dostoevsky's final and greatest novel — a passionate philosophical novel set in 19th-century Russia that enters deeply into the ethical debates of God, free will, and morality.
preview: Alexei Fyodorovich Karamazov was the third son of a landowner from our district, Fyodor Pavlovich Karamazov.
fullText:
Part I, Book I: The History of a Family

Alexei Fyodorovich Karamazov was the third son of a landowner from our district, Fyodor Pavlovich Karamazov, well known in his own day — and he is remembered among us to this day — for his tragic and obscure death, which happened exactly thirteen years ago.

Fyodor Pavlovich Karamazov was a strange type, yet one pretty frequently met with, precisely the type of man who is not only worthless and depraved but muddleheaded as well — one of those muddleheaded people who still handle their own little business affairs quite skilfully.

Part I, Book II: An Inappropriate Gathering

Alexei or Alyosha, as everyone called him, was a true believer — and not one of those who believe only in a certain vague sense, but one of those for whom faith saturated every moment of life.

title: Siddhartha
author: Hermann Hesse
description: A novel of self-discovery set in ancient India. The story of Siddhartha, a young man who leaves his comfortable home to seek spiritual enlightenment along the banks of a great river.
preview: In the shade of the house, in the sunshine on the riverbank near the boats, in the shade of the sal forest, in the shade of the fig tree is where Siddhartha grew up.
fullText:
Part One: The Brahmin's Son

In the shade of the house, in the sunshine on the riverbank near the boats, in the shade of the sal forest, in the shade of the fig tree is where Siddhartha grew up, the handsome son of the Brahmin, the young falcon, together with his friend Govinda.

His shoulders were broad, his eyes deep and brown, his nose long and handsome. His mouth was fine, his forehead high. He was the darling of all, a joy to all.

But Siddhartha was not at peace with himself. He had learned all that the Brahmins knew. But was that enough? He decided to leave.

Part Two: With the Samanas

That night, Siddhartha and Govinda slept in the hut of a ferryman by the river. The next morning Govinda followed his friend. They joined the wandering samanas.

title: The Picture of Dorian Gray
author: Oscar Wilde
description: Oscar Wilde's only novel — a Gothic tale of vanity and corruption. A young man's portrait ages in his place while he remains eternally young, his soul growing darker with every sin.
preview: The studio was filled with the rich odour of roses, and when the light summer wind stirred amidst the trees of the garden, there came through the open door the heavy scent of the lilac.
fullText:
Chapter 1

The studio was filled with the rich odour of roses, and when the light summer wind stirred amidst the trees of the garden, there came through the open door the heavy scent of the lilac, or the more delicate perfume of the pink-flowering thorn.

From the corner of the divan of Persian saddle-bags on which he was lying, smoking, as was his custom, innumerable cigarettes, Lord Henry Wotton could just catch the gleam of the honey-sweet and honey-coloured blossoms of a laburnum.

Chapter 2

Dorian Gray's beauty was extraordinary. Every man who painted him loved him. Lord Henry Wotton saw that, and he used it. He told Dorian that beauty was the only thing worth having. And Dorian believed him, with consequences no one could have foreseen.

title: Wuthering Heights
author: Emily Brontë
description: A wild and passionate story of the doomed love between Catherine Earnshaw and the orphan Heathcliff on the Yorkshire moors — one of the most tempestuous love stories in English literature.
preview: 1801 — I have just returned from a visit to my landlord — the solitary neighbour that I shall be troubled with.
fullText:
Chapter 1

1801 — I have just returned from a visit to my landlord — the solitary neighbour that I shall be troubled with. This is certainly a beautiful country! In all England, I do not believe that I could have fixed on a situation so completely removed from the stir of society.

A perfect misanthropist's Heaven: and Mr. Heathcliff and I are such a suitable pair to divide the desolation between us.

Chapter 2

Mrs. Dean gave me her account. Catherine Earnshaw grew up on the moors with her brother Hindley and the foundling Heathcliff, who Mr. Earnshaw had brought home from Liverpool. The love between Catherine and Heathcliff was wild and fierce — not tamed by society or sense.

title: Don Quixote
author: Miguel de Cervantes
description: Considered the first modern novel and one of the greatest works of fiction ever written. The story of a man who reads so many chivalric romances that he decides to become a knight-errant.
preview: In a village of La Mancha, the name of which I have no desire to call to mind, there lived not long since one of those gentlemen that keep a lance in the lance-rack.
fullText:
Chapter 1

In a village of La Mancha, the name of which I have no desire to call to mind, there lived not long since one of those gentlemen that keep a lance in the lance-rack, an old buckler, a lean hack, and a greyhound for coursing.

A stew of rather more beef than mutton, a salad on most nights, scraps on Saturdays, lentils on Fridays, and a pigeon or so extra on Sundays, made away with three-quarters of his income.

Chapter 2

Having made these preparations, he did not wish to wait any longer in putting his design into execution, impelled to this by the thought of what the world was losing by his delay, seeing what wrongs he intended to right, grievances to redress, injustices to repair, abuses to remove, and duties to discharge.

title: The Trial
author: Franz Kafka
description: Josef K is arrested for an unspecified crime by an unspecified authority and must navigate an incomprehensible legal system. Kafka's eerily prescient vision of bureaucratic absurdity.
preview: Someone must have been telling lies about Josef K., for without having done anything wrong, he was arrested one fine morning.
fullText:
Chapter 1: Arrest — Conversation with Mrs. Grubach, then Fräulein Bürstner

Someone must have been telling lies about Josef K., for without having done anything wrong, he was arrested one fine morning. His landlady Mrs. Grubach's cook always brought him his breakfast at eight o'clock, but today she didn't come.

That had never happened before. K. waited a little while, looked from his pillow at the old woman who lived opposite and who was watching him with a curiosity quite unusual even for her, and then, both hungry and disconcerted, rang the bell.

Chapter 2: First Interrogation

K. was notified by telephone that on the following Sunday a small inquiry into his case would take place. Where was he to go? K. found the address in a street on the edge of the city.

title: Narcopolis
author: Jeet Thayil
description: Shortlisted for the Booker Prize 2012. Set in the opium dens of 1970s Bombay, a searing novel of addiction, loss, and the city's underworld — a Bombay that no longer exists.
preview: Bombay, which obliterated its own history by changing its name and surgically altering its face, is the hero or villain of this story.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Bombay, which obliterated its own history by changing its name and surgically altering its face, is the hero or villain of this story, and since Bombay is a city of strong opinions, this is the city's own account of itself.

I arrived in this city and I found myself in a pipe den on Shuklaji Street, in the time before beer bars and call centres, when the only way to blot out the city's heat and noise was the thin smoke of an opium pipe.

Chapter 2

Rumi worked in the pipe den. She was taller than most men, moved slowly, and spoke in a low voice that carried across the room without effort. She had been born a man and had become herself through a combination of hormones, will, and suffering.

title: Space and Other Stories
author: Upamanyu Chatterjee
description: English August and its sequels trace the absurd life of Agastya Sen, a young IAS officer posted to rural India — a darkly comic portrait of the Indian bureaucracy.
preview: Agastya Sen was twenty-four years old when the service claimed him.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Agastya Sen was twenty-four years old when the service claimed him. He had passed the IAS examination with a rank just high enough to get him into the administrative service, and his first posting was to a town called Madna in the interior of some state he could barely name.

He had never wanted to be a civil servant. He had simply drifted into the examination as a way of deferring a decision about his life, and then to his mild astonishment, he had passed.

Chapter 2

Madna was a town of forty thousand people, a district headquarters, a place with a collector's bungalow and a civil lines and a local dak bungalow and an extraordinary heat.

title: The Inheritance of Loss
author: Kiran Desai
description: Winner of the 2006 Booker Prize. A story of a failed colonial judge and his orphaned granddaughter in the Himalayas, against the backdrop of the Nepali insurgency of the 1980s.
preview: All day, the colours had been those of dusk, mist moving like a shawl across the face of the mountain.
fullText:
Chapter 1

All day, the colours had been those of dusk, mist moving like a shawl across the face of the mountain. The light reached them for only a few hours, and then it was retreating again.

In the kitchen house, Sai sat reading by the dying fire. The cook, her guardian's servant, was chopping onions. The judge, her grandfather, had gone to his room after dinner.

They lived near Kalimpong, in a house called Cho Oyu that the judge had named after the mountain in the Himalayas.

Chapter 2

Biju had been in America for two years. He worked in restaurants — washing dishes, cutting vegetables, mopping floors. He sent money home. Not much, but regularly.

title: Moth Smoke
author: Mohsin Hamid
description: Set in 1990s Lahore, a fired banker drifts into crime and drugs while his wealthy best friend soars. A sharp examination of class, corruption, and desire in Pakistan.
preview: I'm going to tell you a love story, although it's also a story about drugs, crime, poverty, and class.
fullText:
Chapter 1

I'm going to tell you a love story, although it's also a story about drugs, crime, poverty, and class.

Darashikoh Shezad — Daru to his friends — was fired from his bank job on a Tuesday morning in June. He had been a banker for three years, since he left university. He was good at his job, or he had been.

The problem, his manager told him politely, was his attitude. Daru had been coming in late, leaving early, and responding to clients with what could only be described as contempt.

Chapter 2

Aurangzeb came back from America driving a new Toyota Land Cruiser. He was Daru's best friend from school, and now he was everything Daru was not: married, wealthy, successful.

title: Kim
author: Rudyard Kipling
description: The coming-of-age story of Kimball O'Hara, an Irish orphan who grows up on the streets of Lahore. A rich portrait of the India of the Great Game, with all its contradictions.
preview: He sat, in defiance of municipal by-laws, astride the gun Zam-Zammah on her brick platform opposite the old Ajaib-Gher — the Wonder House, as the natives call the Lahore Museum.
fullText:
Chapter 1

He sat, in defiance of municipal by-laws, astride the gun Zam-Zammah on her brick platform opposite the old Ajaib-Gher — the Wonder House, as the natives call the Lahore Museum.

Who hold Zam-Zammah, that 'fire-breathing dragon,' hold the Punjab, for the great green-bronze piece is always first of the conqueror's loot.

There was some justification for Kim — he had kicked Lala Dinanath's boy off the trunnions — since the English held the Punjab and Kim was English. Though he was burned black as any native, though he spoke the vernacular by preference, though he consorted on terms of perfect equality with the small boys of the bazaar, Kim was white.

Chapter 2

He followed the lama out of the museum, half-amused and half-curious. The old man was from Tibet — a lama of high standing.

title: The Remains of the Night
author: Intizar Hussain
description: A lyrical novel about Partition, nostalgia, and the loss of a world. A writer of extraordinary sensitivity explores the long aftermath of 1947 on the Urdu literary imagination.
preview: The city was divided. The people were divided. Even memory itself seemed to have been partitioned.
fullText:
Chapter 1

The city was divided. The people were divided. Even memory itself seemed to have been partitioned.

Basti begins with a man named Zakir who watches as everything he has known is taken from him — first by Partition, then by time, then by his own forgetting.

He had grown up in Rupnagar, a town that no longer existed in the way he remembered it. The streets were the same but their names had changed. The mosque was the same but its congregation had changed.

Chapter 2

His mother would tell him stories of the old town at night. The stories were always about abundance — about the mango gardens, about the rivers, about the festivals.

title: The Ministry of Utmost Happiness
author: Arundhati Roy
description: Roy's long-awaited second novel — a sweeping, polyphonic story that moves from the crowded streets of Old Delhi to the mountains of Kashmir, exploring conflict, love, and the margins of society.
preview: She lived in the graveyard like a tree. At dawn she saw the crows come in from all directions to roost in the pipal tree.
fullText:
Chapter 1

She lived in the graveyard like a tree. At dawn she saw the crows come in from all directions to roost in the pipal tree. She observed their politics, their strutting and jostling, their love affairs and intrigues.

Anjum had not always lived in the graveyard. She had come there after a journey of some years — a journey that had taken her through violence and survival and ultimately to this quiet place.

Chapter 2
