Tilo was an architect who had abandoned architecture. She had loved a man who loved Kashmir more than he loved her. She had followed him there and found the valley in the middle of a war.

title: Invisible Man
author: Ralph Ellison
description: A landmark of American literature. An unnamed narrator describes his journey through a racist society, invisible not because of any supernatural power but because people refuse to see him.
preview: I am an invisible man. No, I am not a spook like those who haunted Edgar Allan Poe; nor am I one of your Hollywood-movie ectoplasms.
fullText:
Prologue

I am an invisible man. No, I am not a spook like those who haunted Edgar Allan Poe; nor am I one of your Hollywood-movie ectoplasms. I am a man of substance, of flesh and bone, fibre and liquids — and I might even be said to possess a mind. I am invisible, understand, simply because people refuse to see me.

Chapter 1

It goes a long way back, some twenty years. All my life I had been looking for something, and everywhere I turned someone tried to tell me what it was. I accepted their answers too, though they were often in contradiction and even self-contradictory.

title: Beloved
author: Toni Morrison
description: A Pulitzer Prize-winning novel about Sethe, an escaped enslaved woman in post-Civil War Ohio, haunted literally and figuratively by the ghost of her dead daughter.
preview: 124 was spiteful. Full of a baby's venom. The women of 124 had suffered and wept.
fullText:
Part One

124 was spiteful. Full of a baby's venom. The women of 124 had suffered and wept and wept and wept. The grandmother, Baby Suggs, was dead, and the sons, Howard and Buglar, had run away by the time they were thirteen years old.

Now the grandmother was dead and the sons were gone and Sethe and her daughter Denver were alone in the house on Bluestone Road.

Sethe had been married to a man named Halle. He was still enslaved when she escaped. She had never heard from him again.

Part Two

Beloved came out of the water. She walked up from the creek, fully clothed, soaked through, and sat down under the tree. Sethe's dog, Here Boy, fled at the sight of her.

title: The Road
author: Cormac McCarthy
description: A Pulitzer Prize-winning post-apocalyptic novel. A father and son travel through a burned America with nothing but each other and the will to survive.
preview: When he woke in the woods in the dark and the cold of the night he'd reach out to touch the child sleeping beside him.
fullText:
Chapter 1

When he woke in the woods in the dark and the cold of the night he'd reach out to touch the child sleeping beside him. Nights dark beyond darkness and the days more gray each one than what had gone before. Like the onset of some cold glaucoma dimming away the world.

His word is truth. He's a man of his word. His word is truth. The man had a pistol with which he held two rounds. Two rounds only. He knew what they were for.

Chapter 2

They went on. The darkness was not total but it was deep and the cold was bitter. The boy did not complain. He never complained. His father wondered sometimes what he was carrying and whether it was worth carrying.

title: Gora
author: Rabindranath Tagore
description: One of Tagore's greatest novels, exploring Indian identity, nationalism, religion, and self-discovery through the character of Gora — a passionate young man who must confront the truth about himself.
preview: Gora walked through the narrow lanes of Kolkata with the certainty of a man who knows who he is. But he did not yet know the thing that would unmake him.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Gora walked through the narrow lanes of Kolkata with the certainty of a man who knows who he is. He was tall, fair-skinned — unusual for a Bengali — and possessed of a passionate nationalist fire that made him beloved among his friends and infuriating to others.

His closest friend Binoy watched him with a mixture of admiration and mild exasperation. Gora was too certain about everything. One day, Binoy suspected, the world would teach Gora something he did not know.

Chapter 2

It was Binoy who first met Sucharita. She was the daughter of Paresh Babu, a Brahmo who had no interest in argument but great interest in inner truth. Sucharita was like her father in that way.

title: Coolie
author: Mulk Raj Anand
description: The story of Munoo, a poor hill boy who moves from domestic service to factory work in 1930s India. Anand's searing novel of class oppression and colonial exploitation.
preview: Munoo came down from the hills to make his fortune. He was fifteen years old and full of hope.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Munoo came down from the hills to make his fortune. He was fifteen years old, a strong boy from a village in the foothills of the Himalayas. He had heard stories of the cities below — of money, of food, of life.

His first job was as a domestic servant in a bank clerk's house in Sham Nagar. The pay was four rupees a month and all meals. He thought this was wealth.

Chapter 2

The mistress of the house was a nervous woman who shouted at everyone. The master was a small man who shouted at no one but let his wife shout at everything. Between them, Munoo learned his first lessons about power.

title: Untouchable
author: Mulk Raj Anand
description: The story of one day in the life of Bakha, a young sweeper from India's lowest caste. A passionate indictment of the caste system and one of the first great Indian novels in English.
preview: The outcastes' colony was a group of mud-walled houses that clustered together in two rows, under the shadow of the town of Bulandshahr.
fullText:
Chapter 1

The outcastes' colony was a group of mud-walled houses that clustered together in two rows, under the shadow of the town of Bulandshahr, in the dead end of a lane that wound through the labyrinthine, tortuous bazaar.

Bakha was sweeper by birth and by profession. He worked for the army barracks near the town. Every morning, at four o'clock, he was up to begin his round of work.

Chapter 2

Bakha had never thought much about the words 'untouchable' and 'low caste' until the day when he accidentally touched a high-caste Hindu in the bazaar. The man's reaction was the first time Bakha really understood what those words meant.

title: The World's Wife
author: Carol Ann Duffy
description: A witty and subversive poetry collection that gives voice to the unnamed women behind famous men in history and myth — from Medusa to Mrs. Darwin.
preview: Little Red Cap: At childhood's end, the houses petered out into playing fields, the factory, allotments kept, like secrets, weed-choked, hidden, the silent railway line.
fullText:
Little Red Cap

At childhood's end, the houses petered out
into playing fields, the factory, allotments
kept, like secrets, weed-choked, hidden, the silent railway line,
then the breeze-blocked, concrete-floored, chemical-stank
of the lock-up garages, the casual cruelty of teenage boys.

Medusa

A suspicion, a doubt, a jealousy
grew in my mind,
which turned the hairs on my head to filthy snakes,
as though my thoughts
hissed and spat on my scalp.

Mrs Quasimodo

Before he came
my loneliness was just a word
I heard folk use
for something I had never felt.

title: Longitude
author: Dava Sobel
description: The gripping story of the greatest scientific problem of the 18th century: how to determine longitude at sea. An English clockmaker named John Harrison solved it and was nearly robbed of the credit.
preview: The chronometer that changed the world was built by a man who had no formal education in clockmaking.
fullText:
Chapter 1: Imaginary Lines

The chronometer that changed the world was built by a man who had no formal education in clockmaking.

John Harrison was a carpenter from Lincolnshire who taught himself to repair and then to build clocks. He became, in the process, the finest horologist of his age — possibly of any age.

Chapter 2: The Sea's Claim

For most of human history, a ship's longitude at sea was a matter of educated guesswork. The consequences of bad guesses were measured in lives.

On October 22, 1707, a naval disaster of the first order occurred when four ships under Admiral Sir Clowdisley Shovell struck the rocks off the Scilly Isles. Two thousand men drowned.

title: The Night Manager
author: John le Carré
description: An ex-soldier turned hotel night manager is recruited by British intelligence to infiltrate the inner circle of a global arms dealer. A gripping thriller of seduction, loyalty and betrayal.
preview: Jonathan Pine was the night manager of a large Swiss hotel when the British agent came to him.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Jonathan Pine was the night manager of a large Swiss hotel when the British agent came to him with a file and a request.

The file was about a man named Richard Onslow Roper, who travelled the world and sold weapons to people who used them on other people. The agent's name was Leonard Burr and he worked for a branch of intelligence that had been largely forgotten.

Chapter 2

Roper himself arrived at the hotel six months after Burr. He had the ease of a man who had never needed to count money. He wore his wealth like a well-fitted coat — present but not obtrusive.

Pine watched him. Pine was good at watching. It was what he had always done, since the army, since before.

title: Shantaram
author: Gregory David Roberts
description: Based on a true story: an escaped Australian convict finds himself in 1980s Bombay, living in the slums, working for the mafia, and falling in love with a city that becomes his destiny.
preview: It took me a long time and most of the world to learn what I know about love and fate and the choices we make.
fullText:
Chapter 1

It took me a long time and most of the world to learn what I know about love and fate and the choices we make, but the heart of it came to me in an instant, while I was chained to a wall and being tortured.

I escaped from an Australian prison in the early 1980s. I lost a great many things in the years that followed, and I found others. I became a free man, and eventually came to Bombay.

Chapter 2

Bombay received me. That was how it felt — as if the city was opening its enormous arms and drawing me into itself. The noise, the colour, the crowds, the energy — it was overwhelming. I stood on the street outside the ferry terminal and I could not move for the wonder of it.

title: The Space Between Us
author: Thrity Umrigar
description: The story of two women in Bombay: Sera, a wealthy Parsi housewife, and her servant Bhima — a devastating portrait of the class divide that separates them even as their lives intertwine.
preview: The space between Sera and Bhima was the space between their worlds — unbridgeable, vast, and deeply felt by both.
fullText:
Chapter 1

The space between Sera and Bhima was the space between their worlds — unbridgeable, vast, and deeply felt by both.

Sera Dubash was a Parsi woman in her mid-fifties, the wife of a retired businessman. She lived in a large flat in Warden Road and employed a cook, a driver, and Bhima.

Bhima had worked for Sera for over twenty years. She came every morning at seven and left at two. She swept, she mopped, she cut vegetables, she washed.

Chapter 2

Bhima's husband had left her. Her son had died of drink. Her daughter Maya, at nineteen, had come home pregnant and unmarried. Bhima supported them all on her seven hundred rupees a month.

title: Games at Twilight
author: Anita Desai
description: Anita Desai's acclaimed story collection capturing the texture of Indian life — childhood, family, loss, and the particular loneliness of middle-class urban India.
preview: The long afternoon unrolled itself before the children like a brown carpet of heat.
fullText:
Games at Twilight

The long afternoon unrolled itself before the children like a brown carpet of heat. The sun pressed down on the earth, and in the burning silence even the crows had gone silent.

The children had been kept inside all afternoon. Their mother had said: too hot, too bright, too dangerous. But now she let them out into the garden.

Ravi was the youngest. He was always the one who hid and was never found, the one who waited in dark places while the others forgot him.

A Devoted Son

When the results of the examinations were announced, Rakesh ran to tell his parents. His mother wept and his father fell silent, then beamed.

title: The Woman in White
author: Wilkie Collins
description: One of the first mystery novels, and still among the best. The story of a strange encounter on a moonlit road, a woman in white, and a conspiracy of secrets that threatens two women's lives.
preview: This is the story of what a Woman's patience can endure, and what a Man's resolution can achieve.
fullText:
Preamble

This is the story of what a Woman's patience can endure, and what a Man's resolution can achieve.

If the machinery of the Law could be depended on to fathom every case of suspicion, and to conduct every process of inquiry, with moderate assistance only from the lubricating influence of oil of gold, the events which fill these pages might have claimed their share of the public attention in a Court of Justice.

Chapter 1: The Story begun by Walter Hartright

It was the last day of July. The long hot summer was drawing to a close; and we who dwelt in the neighbourhood of Hampstead were enjoying the cool of the evening.

title: Rebecca
author: Daphne du Maurier
description: A young woman marries a wealthy widower and comes to live at his magnificent estate, Manderley — only to find herself haunted by the presence of his beautiful dead first wife.
preview: Last night I dreamt I went to Manderley again.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Last night I dreamt I went to Manderley again. It seemed to me I stood by the iron gate leading to the drive, and for a while I could not enter, for the way was barred to me. There was a padlock and a chain upon the gate.

I called in my dream to the lodge-keeper, and had no answer, and peering closer through the rusted spokes of the gate I saw that the lodge was uninhabited.

Chapter 2

I met Maxim de Winter in Monte Carlo. He was forty-two years old. I was twenty-one. He was a widower of great standing and I was the companion of a rich American woman who treated me as an afterthought.

title: Pachinko
author: Min Jin Lee
description: A multigenerational epic about a Korean family in Japan across the 20th century — a sweeping story of sacrifice, ambition, love, and identity that asks what it means to belong.
preview: History has failed us, but no matter.
fullText:
Book One: Gohyang / Hometown, 1910–1933

History has failed us, but no matter.

In the beginning was a line. A line between those who had names and those who did not. Between those who had land and those who worked on it.

Sunja's father ran a boarding house near the docks in Yeongdo. He had been born with a cleft palate and a limp, and he was the kindest man she knew.

Book Two: Motherland, 1939–1962

Yoseb and Isak had found their way to Osaka through a combination of luck and determination. Isak was a minister. Yoseb was a factory worker. Together they managed, barely.

title: The Great Indian Novel
author: Shashi Tharoor
description: A satirical retelling of the Mahabharata as the story of modern India's political history, from independence to the Emergency. Tharoor's brilliantly funny and wickedly sharp novel.
preview: The story of modern India told through the ancient text of the Mahabharata — because every story in India has already been told before.
fullText:
Chapter 1: In the Beginning

The story of modern India told through the ancient text of the Mahabharata — because every story in India has already been told before.

Gangaji was the Bhishma of our age: the grand old man who renounced the throne and lived to see what his renunciation had made possible and what it had made impossible.

Mokrasi was both a sage and a fool. He had lived through it all. He had seen Gandhi's arrival in South Africa, his return to India, the salt march, the Quit India movement, independence, Partition.

Chapter 2: The Princes and the Princes

Duryodhani was the villain of the piece. She was the Prime Minister who bent the constitution to her will. Her hundred sons were the ministers she appointed and the courts she packed.

title: Normal People
author: Sally Rooney
description: The story of Connell and Marianne, who grow up in the same small town in Ireland but inhabit different worlds — and whose complex, passionate relationship follows them to university and beyond.
preview: Marianne stood at the sink rinsing a coffee cup. The window looked out over the back garden and the housing estate beyond.
fullText:
January 2012

Marianne stood at the sink rinsing a coffee cup. The window looked out over the back garden and the housing estate beyond. She had a book folded open on the counter beside her, one she'd been reading since the morning.

Connell's mother worked for Marianne's family, cleaning their house. He came sometimes after school to pick her up. He and Marianne were in the same year at school.

At school Marianne was difficult. Odd. Everyone knew it. She said things without thinking about how they would land.

February 2012

Connell sat with the boys at lunch. They talked about girls. He didn't say anything about Marianne. It was not possible to say anything about Marianne without it becoming a thing.

title: The Shadow of the Wind
author: Carlos Ruiz Zafón
description: Set in post-Civil War Barcelona, a young boy discovers a mysterious novel, and his search for its author leads him into a labyrinth of secrets, danger, and forbidden love.
preview: I still remember the day my father took me to the Cemetery of Forgotten Books for the first time.
fullText:
Chapter 1: The Cemetery of Forgotten Books

I still remember the day my father took me to the Cemetery of Forgotten Books for the first time. It was the early summer of 1945, and we walked through the streets of a Barcelona trapped beneath ashen skies.

The Cemetery of Forgotten Books was a place where old books came to wait. My father said each book had a soul — the soul of the person who had written it and of those who had read it.

I chose a book that morning. It was called The Shadow of the Wind by Julián Carax. I did not know then that the book would change my life.

Chapter 2

As the years passed, I tried to find other novels by Carax. But they were gone — someone had been buying and burning every copy of every book Carax ever wrote.

title: Stoner
author: John Williams
description: Perhaps the most quietly devastating novel in American literature. The story of William Stoner, a farmer's son who becomes a literature professor — a life of quiet passion and failure.
preview: William Stoner entered the University of Missouri as a freshman in the year 1910, at the age of nineteen.
fullText:
Chapter 1

William Stoner entered the University of Missouri as a freshman in the year 1910, at the age of nineteen. Eight years later, during the height of World War I, he received his Doctor of Philosophy degree and accepted an instructorship at the same University, where he taught until his death in 1956.

He did not rise above the rank of assistant professor, and few students remembered him with any sharpness after they had taken his courses. When he died his colleagues made a memorial contribution of a medieval manuscript to the University library. This was made in his name.

Chapter 2

Stoner was the son of a farmer. The farm was poor and the soil was thin. His father and mother had worked the land for thirty years without ever quite making it pay.

title: A Man Called Ove
author: Fredrik Backman
description: A grumpy old man in Sweden who has decided to end his life finds himself pulled back into the world by his new neighbours. A life-affirming story of love, grief, and unexpected friendship.
preview: Ove is fifty-nine years old and has lived in the same house for forty years. He knows exactly what is right and exactly what is wrong.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Ove is fifty-nine years old and has lived in the same house for forty years. He knows exactly what is right and exactly what is wrong in the world, and he has no patience for those who do not know.

He walks around the neighbourhood every morning, making sure everything is as it should be. Cars parked in the right places. Bicycles locked up correctly. The sign by the gate showing the right speed.

His wife Sonja died six months ago. Since then, Ove has had a plan.

Chapter 2

The new neighbours arrived with a trailer and started reversing over the flower beds. Ove watched from his window and felt the familiar surge of righteous exasperation rise in his chest.

title: The Unbearable Lightness of Being
author: Milan Kundera
description: Set during the Prague Spring of 1968, Kundera's masterpiece explores the relationship between two couples through the lens of love, infidelity, and political repression.
preview: The idea of eternal return is a mysterious one, and Nietzsche has often perplexed other philosophers with it.
fullText:
Part One: Lightness and Weight

The idea of eternal return is a mysterious one, and Nietzsche has often perplexed other philosophers with it. To think that everything recurs as we once experienced it, and that the recurrence itself recurs ad infinitum!

What does this strange myth signify? The myth of eternal return states that a life which disappears once and for all, which does not return, is like a shadow, without weight, dead in advance, and whether it was horrible, beautiful, or sublime, its horror, sublimity, and beauty mean nothing.

Part Two: Soul and Body

Tomas worked as a surgeon in Prague. He was fifty and had been divorced from his first wife for ten years. He lived alone in a large flat and took many lovers but loved none of them until Tereza arrived with a book under her arm.

title: The Buddha in the Attic
author: Julie Otsuka
description: Told in a collective first person, this lyrical novella follows Japanese picture brides who cross the Pacific to marry strangers in early 20th-century California.
preview: On the boat we were mostly virgins. We had left our husbands with silk fans and white fingers.
fullText:
Come, Japanese

On the boat we were mostly virgins. We had left our husbands with silk fans and white fingers and they had been nothing like we had imagined them to be.

We were sixteen and seventeen years old. We were from the mountains and the valleys and the rice paddies of Kyushu. We had never worn shoes before. We spoke in a language our new husbands would not understand.

First Night

On the first night our new husbands took us quickly. They were lonely men who had been on the farm for years without the company of women and they could not wait.

Some of us cried. Some of us bit our lips. Some of us were surprised to find that the act was familiar from the animals we had watched in the fields back home.

title: Small Things Like These
author: Claire Keegan
description: A quiet, devastating novella set in 1980s Ireland. A coal merchant begins to question the secrets his town keeps, particularly about the local convent and the women kept within its walls.
preview: There was a time when Bill Furlong was a boy and this town had no convent and there was no reason to be afraid of Christmas.
fullText:
Chapter 1

There was a time when Bill Furlong was a boy and this town had no convent and there was no reason to be afraid of Christmas. He remembered it in the way people remember times before some fundamental change — with a nostalgia that was also a sense of loss.

Bill was a coal merchant now, with a wife and five daughters and a small but stable business. He was not a complicated man. He liked order and repetition. He liked delivering coal to the same houses every winter.

Chapter 2

It was December when Bill heard the sound from the convent yard. A girl's voice, calling out. He stopped his cart. He listened. Then he went into the yard.

title: The Blue Umbrella
author: Ruskin Bond
description: Set in the hills of Garhwal, a young girl trades a family keepsake for a beautiful blue umbrella — and the umbrella becomes the most coveted object in the village. A tender and warm Indian tale.
preview: There was a blue umbrella that became the most important thing in the village.
fullText:
Chapter 1

Biniya's grandmother had a small piece of ivory with a carving of the sacred cow etched into it. She had received it as a young woman, and she wore it around her neck on a black thread.

One afternoon, some tourists came through the village on their way to the mountain. One of them stopped and looked at the old woman's necklace.

'I'll trade you for it,' said the tourist, holding up a blue umbrella. It was the most beautiful shade of blue that Biniya had ever seen.

Chapter 2

Ram Bharosa, who kept the tea shop, coveted the umbrella from the moment he first saw it. He offered Biniya all sorts of things: fresh parathas every day, a warm jacket, a new pair of shoes.

title: Night of the Scorpion
author: Nissim Ezekiel
description: Ezekiel's celebrated poetry collection that established him as the founder of modern Indian English poetry. Moving between Bombay, memory, and identity with intelligence and gentle irony.
preview: I remember the night my mother was stung by a scorpion. Ten hours of steady rain had driven him to crawl beneath a sack of rice.
fullText:
Night of the Scorpion

I remember the night my mother
was stung by a scorpion. Ten hours
of steady rain had driven him
to crawl beneath a sack of rice.

Parting

So we must say Goodbye, my darling,
And go, as lovers go, for ever;
Tonight remains, to pack and fix on labels
And make an end of lying down together.

Enterprise

It started as a pilgrimage,
An idyll dreamed in schoolboy days,
A journey to our source,
All things considered. But the terrain
Proved hostile.

title: Tender is the Night
author: F. Scott Fitzgerald
description: Set on the French Riviera in the 1920s, the story of Dick Diver — a brilliant psychiatrist who falls into dissipation. Fitzgerald's most ambitious novel, and perhaps his most personal.
preview: On the pleasant shore of the French Riviera, about half way between Marseilles and the Italian border, stands a large, proud, rose-colored hotel.
fullText:
Book One, Chapter 1

On the pleasant shore of the French Riviera, about half way between Marseilles and the Italian border, stands a large, proud, rose-colored hotel. Deferential palms cool its flushed facade, and before it stretches a short dazzling beach.

Rosemary was eighteen years old, ripe but still uncomfortable in her ripeness. She had been on a year's tour with her mother, visiting the capitals of Europe.

Book One, Chapter 2

Dick Diver and his wife Nicole held court on the beach every afternoon. They were the center of the small colony of Americans and English who had discovered this part of the Riviera before everyone else did.

title: The Vegetarian
author: Han Kang
description: A Man Booker International Prize winner. After a disturbing dream, Yeong-hye decides to stop eating meat — a decision that unleashes violence and obsession from those around her.
preview: Before my wife turned vegetarian, I'd always thought of her as completely unremarkable in every way.
fullText:
Part One: The Vegetarian

Before my wife turned vegetarian, I'd always thought of her as completely unremarkable in every way. To be frank, the first time I met her I wasn't even attracted to her. She was a passive, timid, uneventful woman.

She prepared balanced meals. She kept the house clean. She rarely got sick.

So when I woke up one morning and found all the meat gone from the fridge, I thought it was a mistake.

Part Two: Mongolian Mark

Her brother-in-law was an artist. He had always found Yeong-hye beautiful, in a way he had tried not to think about. When he heard about her refusal to eat, something in his mind unlocked.

title: River of Smoke
author: Amitav Ghosh
description: The second book in Ghosh's acclaimed Ibis trilogy. Ships and lives converge in 1838 Canton as the opium trade reaches its peak — a vivid panorama of a world on the edge of catastrophe.
preview: The River of Smoke begins where the Sea of Poppies ends — with the great cyclone over the Bay of Bengal.
fullText:
Part One: Mauritius

The River of Smoke begins where the Sea of Poppies ends — with the great cyclone over the Bay of Bengal.

Deeti was on the island of Mauritius. She had survived the shipwreck and made a new life there, but her mind was always returning to the ships and the sea and the people she had left behind.

Part Two: Canton

Canton in 1838 was the most cosmopolitan city in the world — a place where Chinese merchants, European traders, Indian opium dealers, and American entrepreneurs jostled together in a narrow strip of factories along the Pearl River.

Bahram Modi had come from Bombay with a cargo of Malwa opium. He had been coming to Canton for years. He knew every merchant, every linguist, every shroff.
